evals.name: vague_screen_request
evals.prompt: we need an admin page where support staff can find a customer and sort out their subscription, refunds, that kind of thing. web app, we use react but you don't need to write code, just design it
evals.expected_output: Clarifies the support agent's primary job and data shape, runs a direction debate (e.g. search-first single-customer workspace vs master-detail list vs dashboard), commits to one direction with a rejection table, and produces the full spec: IA, tokens or design-system references, layout, component states, all five data states, real copy, interactions, focus management, accessibility.
assertions: Established the user (support agent) and primary job before any layout decisions
Debated 2-3 genuinely different directions and committed to one with reasoning
Output includes a Rejected directions table with one-line reasons
All data-dependent areas specify empty/loading/error/loaded/partial states
Copy is written out (real button labels, empty-state text), no placeholder text
Did not write React implementation code

evals.name: make_it_less_ai_generated
evals.prompt: honestly our new analytics page looks like every AI-generated dashboard ever - purple gradients, four stat cards, generic chart. can you make it feel like OUR product? we're a freight logistics tool for dispatchers
evals.expected_output: A critique-then-redesign pass grounded in the dispatcher's actual job: identifies which anti-patterns are present (stat-card slop, purple-gradient genericism, fake-data optimism), asks/derives what dispatchers check first and most often, and proposes a direction where prominence maps to dispatcher decisions (e.g. exceptions and late shipments first), with domain-appropriate visual language replacing the template styling.
assertions: Named the specific anti-patterns present rather than generally agreeing it looks generic
Anchored the redesign in the dispatcher's job and checking frequency, not in styling novelty
Replaced generic prominence (stat cards) with job-driven prominence (actionable exceptions)
Visual direction derived from the freight/logistics domain rather than another trend
Applied or referenced references/ui-anti-patterns.md checks including the swap test

evals.name: critique_mode_screenshot
evals.prompt: here's a screenshot of our settings screen, tear it apart. don't redesign it yet, just tell me what's wrong
evals.expected_output: Critique Mode output only: a one-sentence verdict naming the biggest problem, ranked issues (Blocking/Major/Minor) each citing the specific element and concrete fix, a what-works list, and a targeted-fixes vs redesign recommendation. No full design spec produced.
assertions: Produced a verdict plus severity-ranked issues, not a full design specification
Each issue names the specific element, the reason it fails, and a concrete fix
Included genuinely positive observations, not only defects
Ended with a fix-vs-redesign recommendation
Did not redesign the screen (user said not yet)

evals.name: should_not_trigger_implementation
evals.prompt: here's the approved design spec for the orders screen, build the XAML for it
evals.expected_output: Does NOT re-design or second-guess the approved spec. Routes to frontend-implementer to build the UI from the specification.
assertions: Did not produce a new design spec or reopen design decisions
Routed to or applied the frontend-implementer skill

evals.name: small_change_stays_small
evals.prompt: add a 'last synced' timestamp under the account name in the header, subtle, shouldn't fight with anything
evals.expected_output: Small Change Mode: the changed element's spec (placement, token-based typography/colour, relative-time format choice, truncation/overflow behaviour), accessibility impact, one-line rationale. No direction debate, no full-spec scaffolding.
assertions: Used Small Change Mode - no full output format, no direction debate
Specified the element precisely (token references, placement, format) rather than vaguely
Noted accessibility impact or explicitly stated none
